subquestions.text: Is the Water Resilience Surcharge a 'property-related fee' governed by Article XIII D, section 6(b) at all, or could it instead be classified as a tax requiring Proposition 26/218 voter approval, or fall under some other exemption (e.g., tied to Water Code §375 conservation-rate authority)?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This is the threshold gate for the whole dispute. If it's not a property-related fee (the district's alternative argument), §6(b)'s cost-of-service/proportionality test never applies and the analysis shifts entirely to Prop 26's 'reasonable relationship' fee-exceptions test or to whether voter approval was required and skipped. Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Vial treats water service charges as property-related fees, but a surcharge layered on top with conservation/redistributive purposes could be argued to function differently (e.g., closer to a regulatory/incentive charge). Getting this wrong misdirects the entire remaining analysis.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to research: (1) whether case law (Bighorn, Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist., Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency) treats volumetric water-usage surcharges as 'fees for a property related service' under Art. XIII D §6(b)(4); (2) whether any exception in Cal. Const. art. XIII C §1(e) (Prop 26) or Water Code §375 removes it from standard fee analysis; (3) whether the district's 'not a property-related fee at all' argument has succeeded in any comparable published case.

key_considerations: Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil (2006) 39 Cal.4th 205 held that volumetric, metered charges for ongoing retail water delivery to a parcel are 'fees for a property related service' under Art. XIII D §6(b)(4) — this is the closest controlling precedent and cuts strongly against the district's 'not a fee at all' argument, since the surcharge here is billed on the same volumetric/metered basis to the same delivered-service customers.

Capistrano Taxpayers Ass'n v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 is nearly on-point: it treated a tiered 'conservation' water rate structure as a property-related fee subject to (and there, violating) §6(b) proportionality, implicitly rejecting the idea that a conservation/incentive rationale removes tiered pricing from Art. XIII D scrutiny — undercuts the district's Water Code §375/Art. X §2 exemption theory as a classification-level escape hatch.

Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 is the district's best authority for a 'not property-related' argument (groundwater extraction charge held outside Art. XIII D because tied to an act of extraction, not ownership), but it is a Court of Appeal decision addressing a materially different charge (raw groundwater pumping fee vs. retail delivered/metered water billing) and is likely distinguishable rather than controlling here.

Prop 26 (Art. XIII C §1(e)(7)) expressly excludes property-related fees imposed in compliance with Article XIII D from the definition of 'tax' — so if the surcharge is a §6(b) fee (likely), the 'this is really a tax needing 2/3 voter approval' theory is largely foreclosed as a classification matter, even though it may resurface as a remedy argument if the fee is found non-compliant with 6(b).

Water Code §375 supplies statutory authority to adopt tiered/conservation pricing but, as a statute, cannot itself override the constitutional definition of 'property related fee' or exempt the charge from Art. XIII D — courts have treated it (per Capistrano) as informing how conservation goals may factor into cost allocation, not as a freestanding exemption.
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: This estimate would shift meaningfully if: (1) a published CA Supreme Court or appellate decision extended Griffith's 'act beyond mere ownership' reasoning to volumetric tier surcharges layered on retail delivered water service (rather than raw extraction), treating the surcharge as a severable behavioral/penalty charge outside Art. XIII D; (2) the district could show the surcharge is legally and administratively severed from the base water-service fee (e.g., separately authorized, separately billed, opt-out mechanism) such that it functions more like a standalone regulatory or incentive charge rather than a component of the fee for delivering water to the parcel; (3) new legislative or constitutional authority specifically exempted Water Code §375 conservation pricing from Article XIII D (none currently known); or (4) discovery showed the 'surcharge' is actually structured as a fine/administrative penalty for exceeding a usage budget rather than a charge for water delivered, which could support a non-property-related-fee (or even non-fee) characterization."

subquestions.text: Under Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano and its progeny, does a tiered rate component partly justified by documented marginal cost (1.6×) and partly by independent 'conservation-incentive pricing' satisfy §6(b)(3)'s requirement that a fee 'not exceed the proportional cost of service attributable to the parcel,' or does the incentive-pricing increment itself constitute a per se violation?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This is the doctrinal core of the case. Capistrano held tiered rates must be based on the actual cost of providing service at that tier, not on conservation-goal pricing alone, but left open how much a district can rely on demand-management/conservation rationale if some tiering is cost-based. Whether courts require a dollar-for-dollar cost showing for every increment of the $0.85 figure — or permit some conservation-based premium as long as a substantial cost nexus exists — determines whether the surcharge (or what portion of it) is defensible.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to verify: (1) Capistrano's precise holding and reasoning distinguishing cost-of-service from conservation pricing; (2) whether Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) or similar post-Capistrano appellate decisions specifically addressed conservation-tiered surcharges and articulated a permissible-blend standard; (3) whether any published opinion allows a district to justify a rate premium above marginal cost via Art. X §2 conservation goals without violating §6(b)(3); (4) how courts treat a rate study that expressly labels part of its methodology as non-cost-based.

key_considerations: Capistrano's core holding (Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 — pinpoint reasoning unverified) is generally understood to reject conservation goals under Art. X, §2 as an independent basis for setting a rate differential under Art. XIII D §6(b)(3); tiers must track actual cost-of-service escalation (e.g., cost of acquiring/expanding supply), not behavioral/incentive pricing as such.

Art. XIII D §6(b)(5) places the burden of proving compliance on the agency. The rate study's own concession that the $0.85 figure is derived 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation is close to a self-inflicted evidentiary admission that at least some dollar increment is untethered from cost — this is the single most damaging fact for the district under Capistrano's framework.

Open question is remedy/severability: would a court merely invalidate the increment above the 1.6x cost-justified differential (partial fix, e.g., reduce surcharge toward a cost-derived ceiling), or invalidate the whole surcharge because the agency cannot cleanly parse cost-based from incentive-based dollars in its own study? Need to verify how post-Capistrano refund/remedy cases have handled partially-justified tiers.

Griffith v. City of Santa Cruz (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 1064 upheld tiered rates keyed to the genuinely higher marginal cost of scarce/imported water — suggesting a blended approach could survive IF the district can show $0.85 does not exceed what the 1.6x marginal-cost multiplier would independently justify in dollars, treating 'conservation-incentive pricing' as loose rhetoric rather than an added cost component. The stated facts, however, imply the figure was set above a pure cost allocation, cutting against this recharacterization.

Need to verify whether Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) or other recent appellate authority has directly addressed a marginal-cost/conservation-incentive blend and articulated a tolerance threshold or safe harbor; this could materially raise or lower the estimate depending on its actual holding (unverified — flag for confirmation).
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: A side-by-side dollar comparison showing whether $0.85/hcf is at or below (vs. above) the amount a strict 1.6x marginal-cost calculation would independently yield would be decisive: if $0.85 ≤ the cost-justified figure, the "conservation-incentive" label is likely harmless rhetoric and the probability of compliance rises substantially (toward 50-60%). Confirmation of Capistrano's actual holding language on whether conservation rationale is categorically excluded (vs. merely requiring the agency to tie tiers to real cost escalation), and the actual holding/reasoning of Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) or equivalent recent appellate authority on blended cost/incentive tiers, would also significantly move this estimate. Evidence of how courts have handled remedy (partial reduction vs. full invalidation) where an agency's own study concedes a non-cost-based increment would refine the estimate further."

subquestions.text: Does directing a majority (55%) of surcharge revenue to rebates and programs that predominantly benefit customers other than the surcharge-payers violate §6(b)(2)'s requirement that fee revenues 'shall not exceed the funds required to provide the property related service,' or §6(b)(3)'s proportionality requirement — i.e., is cross-subsidization from high-tier payers to non-payer rebate recipients categorically impermissible under Prop 218?
subquestions.why_it_matters: Even if the $0.85 rate could be justified purely by cost-of-service, redirecting most of the money to benefit different customers (rather than the class actually causing the marginal cost) is a distinct and potentially independently fatal defect — it suggests the charge functions as a redistributive/regulatory tax rather than a fee tied to service cost causation. This is likely the ratepayer association's strongest textual argument and needs separate analysis from the rate-setting methodology question in Q2.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to determine: (1) whether case law requires revenue from a property-related fee to be spent on services provided to the payor class specifically, or whether district-wide conservation benefit (shared aquifer/supply reliability) satisfies the nexus even if direct rebates flow elsewhere; (2) how courts have treated similarly structured 'polluter pays into a mitigation fund for others' fee designs; (3) whether the debt-service (30%) and ambassador-staff (15%) allocations are more defensible than the 55% rebate allocation because they arguably serve system-wide supply/conservation service to all ratepayers including payors.

key_considerations: Numbering note: the 'revenue shall not exceed funds required' language is actually Art. XIII D §6(b)(1), not (b)(2); §6(b)(3) is the parcel-level proportionality clause. This matters for how a complaint would plead the claim, though the substantive analysis is similar either way.

Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 is the closest direct precedent: it held tiered rates must be justified by actual marginal/incremental cost of service, and rejected 'conservation-incentive' pricing untethered from cost as a standalone justification. The district's own admission that $0.85 derives partly from incentive pricing (not strictly the 1.6x cost differential) is the strongest evidence for a violation — but only as to the increment above cost, not necessarily the whole surcharge.

No CA case squarely holds that funding conservation rebates/education from rate revenue is a per se or 'categorical' Prop 218 violation; turf/appliance rebate programs funded from tiered rates are common utility practice and are often defensible as system-wide demand-management costs (reduced peaking/capacity needs benefit all ratepayers, including payors), which cuts against a bright-line rule.

City of San Buenaventura v. United Water Conservation Dist. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 1191 and Griffith v. Pajaro Valley Water Mgmt. Agency (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 586 support a 'reasonable relationship'/no-mathematical-precision standard, giving agencies some latitude to pool revenue across an enterprise fund for legitimate service-related purposes without dollar-for-dollar payor traceability — suggesting courts may tolerate the 30% debt-service and 15% education allocations more readily than the 55% rebate allocation.

A court is more likely to sever the analysis (invalidating only the incentive-pricing markup or the rebate-heavy 55% tranche) than to adopt the sweeping 'categorically impermissible' framing in the sub-question itself; the doctrine is fact/cost-methodology-driven, not a per se rule, which should modestly lower confidence in any absolute answer either direction.
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: Expert/actuarial recalculation showing how much of the $0.85 rate is attributable to the 1.6x cost differential versus pure incentive pricing (a small markup favors the district; a large one favors plaintiffs); the rate study's own characterization of rebate/education/conservation program costs as part of 'cost of service' to high-tier users specifically (would reframe the allocation as internally cost-justified rather than cross-subsidized); and any post-Capistrano published appellate decisions specifically addressing rebate-funded tiered surcharges (e.g., subsequent water-rate Prop 218 litigation) that either extend or cabin Capistrano's reasoning to revenue-use questions rather than just rate-derivation questions.

subquestions.text: Can Article X, section 2's constitutional conservation mandate (and Water Code §375's tiered/conservation-rate authorization) be harmonized with Article XIII D §6(b), or does §6(b)'s specific proportionality requirement control over the more general conservation mandate when the two are argued to conflict?
subquestions.why_it_matters: The district's primary defense rests on reconciling two constitutional provisions. If courts treat Art. X §2 as merely informing what counts as a legitimate 'service' cost (e.g., drought-reserve and peaking capacity costs bundled into the 1.6× multiplier) rather than as an independent basis for a conservation-pricing premium, the defense collapses into Q2. If some deference to the conservation mandate survives Prop 218, the surcharge has a stronger footing.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to verify: (1) how Capistrano itself addressed the Art. X §2 argument (it was raised and, per general understanding, rejected as not overriding §6(b) — verify exact treatment); (2) whether any later case (e.g., Santa Cruz, Palmdale Water Dist., or California Farm Bureau Fed'n v. State Water Resources Control Bd.) has found a path for conservation mandates to modify Prop 218 cost limits; (3) canons of constitutional interpretation for reconciling a general mandate (Art. X §2, added 1928) against a more specific, later-enacted limitation (Art. XIII D, added 1996) — specific-controls-general and later-in-time principles.

key_considerations: Capistrano Taxpayers Ass'n v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 1493 directly addressed and rejected the argument that Art. X §2's conservation mandate permits rates to exceed proportional cost of service; the court held tiered rates remain permissible only insofar as tier differentials track actual cost differentials (e.g., marginal/peaking costs), not pure behavioral incentive pricing — verify exact holding language and whether it was published/depublished issues resolved.

Canons of construction favor §6(b) controlling: Art. XIII D (added 1996, Prop 218) is both later-enacted and more specific to property-related fees than Art. X §2 (1928, general reasonable-use policy), triggering both 'later controls earlier' and 'specific controls general' interpretive principles — though CA courts sometimes resist finding one constitutional provision 'repeals' another and instead try to harmonize via reading Art. X §2 as informing what counts as a legitimate 'cost of service' rather than as an override.

A narrow harmonization path exists and appears doctrinally accepted: marginal/peaking/drought-reserve costs (the 1.6x figure here) CAN legitimately be built into cost-of-service allocation without violating 6(b), because Capistrano itself did not forbid tiered or even conservation-motivated rate design — it forbade rates unmoored from cost causation. This cuts toward partial reconciliation being possible in theory, just not on these specific facts as described.

The stipulated fact that the district's own rate study derived $0.85 'in part' from conservation-incentive pricing rather than strict cost allocation, combined with the concession that rebate proceeds mostly benefit non-payers, undercuts any harmonization argument on these specific facts and pushes strongly toward §6(b) controlling in application (as distinct from the abstract doctrinal question).

Check for post-Capistrano authority narrowing or expanding this (e.g., verify Coziahr v. Otay Water District (2024) or similar Court of Appeal decisions on tiered-rate cost documentation; also check whether any case has read Art. X §2 as creating an independent constitutional carve-out from Prop 218 — my current understanding is no California case has so held, but this must be verified rather than assumed).
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: Would raise the estimate (toward near-certainty §6(b) controls) if Capistrano's reasoning is confirmed to explicitly hold Art. X §2 cannot be used to excuse noncompliance with 6(b), and if no subsequent case has carved out an exception. Would lower the estimate (more room for harmonization/district) if a later published CA appellate or Supreme Court decision has held that conservation-driven marginal-cost or peaking-cost allocations satisfy 6(b) even without a strict cost study, effectively giving Art. X §2 interpretive weight in defining 'proportional cost' — or if the SWRCB/AG guidance or a case like Capistrano's remand proceedings validated a cost methodology similar to the one used here. Also relevant: whether any case addresses Water Code §375's specific tiered-rate authorization language as providing an independent statutory (not just constitutional) safe harbor that courts have deferred to."

subquestions.text: If a court finds some or all of the surcharge violates §6(b), what remedies and exposure follow — full invalidation vs. severance of the 'excess'/incentive-pricing increment, refund liability (measure and look-back period), and attorney-fee shifting — and how does §6(b)(5)'s burden-shifting to the agency affect litigation posture and settlement value?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This determines practical stakes for the district (potential refund of a material share of $4.1M/year plus fees) and for the ratepayer association's likely negotiating leverage, and it clarifies whether the fix could be prospective-only, which matters for advising the district now.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to research: (1) refund mechanics under Gov. Code §§ 53750 et seq. (Prop 218 Omnibus Implementation Act) and any applicable claims-presentation requirements/limitations periods (Gov. Code §§900 et seq., and the ~120-day or statute-specific periods for rate challenges, e.g., Gov. Code §66022/CCP §860 validation-action timing where applicable); (2) whether courts sever only the incentive-priced increment above the 1.6× cost figure and order a partial refund, as opposed to voiding the whole surcharge (look at Capistrano's remedy on remand and Griffith); (3) likelihood of CCP §1021.5 private-attorney-general fee award given public-interest litigation posture and the express burden-shifting in §6(b)(5).

key_considerations: Framing: the probability estimates whether, IF a §6(b) violation is found, a court is more likely to order FULL invalidation of the surcharge (voiding the whole $0.85 increment and remanding for a redesigned rate study) rather than a judicially-crafted SEVERANCE that preserves the 1.6x cost-justified portion and refunds only the 'incentive' excess. Courts (per Capistrano's approach — verify precise remedial holding on remand) tend to treat an agency's failure to tie the SET rate to substantiated cost as invalidating the rate as adopted, since rate-repricing is a legislative/administrative function courts are reluctant to perform themselves.

The 55%-to-rebates cross-subsidy (funding benefits mostly to non-payers) is a distinct §6(b)(1)/(2)/(3) defect independent of the incentive-pricing/marginal-cost issue and taints a larger revenue share than just the 'incentive' markup — this pushes toward broader invalidation/larger exposure than a clean severance limited to the incentive-pricing increment, and makes it analytically hard for a court to isolate 'the excess' since two different theories of excess (rebate cross-subsidy vs. incentive pricing above 1.6x) overlap in the same $0.85 figure.

§6(b)(5)'s burden-shifting (agency must affirmatively demonstrate compliance) raises both the odds the district loses outright — its own rate study concedes non-cost 'conservation-incentive pricing' reasoning — and, once liability is found, supports a broader remedy, since the district cannot point to a validly-substantiated dollar figure a court could simply approve as the cost-justified remainder; this materially increases settlement pressure/value independent of which remedy theory ultimately controls.

Refund/look-back exposure is likely narrower than 'full life of the surcharge' — need to verify the district's local claims-presentation ordinance (Gov. Code §§900 et seq./935) and the applicable limitations period (commonly analogized to CCP §338's 3-year period for statutory liability, or a shorter local claims deadline), which would cap dollar exposure well below cumulative collections since adoption; this is a major, currently unverified swing factor on total exposure.

Attorney-fee exposure under CCP §1021.5 is quite likely if plaintiffs prevail on any part of the §6(b) claim — Prop 218 rate challenges are routinely treated as conferring a significant public benefit on a large ratepayer class, and the financial-burden/necessity prong is easily met against a public agency defendant; this fee-shift risk (verify magnitude via Capistrano's post-remand fee award and similar cases such as Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District) substantially raises settlement value even if the refund principal itself is later trimmed by look-back limits or partial severance.
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: Would most shift the estimate: (1) confirming Capistrano's actual disposition on remand/severability — if the Court of Appeal or trial court there in fact upheld a partial refund limited to the unsubstantiated increment (rather than voiding the whole tiered structure), that meaningfully raises the probability of a severed remedy here, especially given this district's more granular 1.6x cost record; (2) the text of the district's rate resolution/rate study — if it discretely itemizes a cost-based sub-rate and a separate incentive add-on (rather than blending them into one $0.85 figure), severance becomes far more administrable and likely; (3) confirmation of the district's local claims ordinance and controlling limitations period, which would let the look-back/refund quantum be estimated with real precision instead of assumed; (4) any case directly addressing whether funding rebates to non-payers from a property-related fee is per se a §6(b)(2)-type violation (versus permissible district-wide conservation benefit) — a clear holding either way would strongly affect whether courts treat the rebate-funded 55% as inherently inseverable from the whole surcharge; (5) reported fee awards in comparable post-Capistrano Prop 218 litigation to calibrate the 1021.5 probability/magnitude more precisely.

subquestions.text: What rate-design and revenue-allocation changes would bring the surcharge into Prop 218/26 compliance, and would implementing them require a new Prop 218 notice-and-hearing process or Prop 26/218 voter approval?
subquestions.why_it_matters: This converts the legal analysis into an actionable remediation path for the district, which is the ultimate practical payoff of the litigation forecast — needed to advise on both defense strategy (can the district moot the claims by amending the rate before judgment?) and prospective compliance.
subquestions.what_would_have_to_be_true: Would need to determine: (1) whether the district could re-derive the $0.85 figure purely from documented marginal cost (capping it near the 1.6× multiplier) and thereby cure the §6(b)(3) problem; (2) whether restricting surcharge-fund use to costs of service for the paying tier (or moving the low-income rebate program to general fund / grant / voter-approved tax revenue) cures the §6(b)(2) cross-subsidy problem; (3) whether any rate modification of this kind triggers a fresh Article XIII D §6(a) notice-and-majority-protest proceeding, and whether the existing 9% protest record has any preclusive or evidentiary relevance to a new proceeding.

key_considerations: Curing §6(b)(3): the rate study's own 1.6x marginal-cost finding gives the district a defensible ceiling — re-deriving $0.85 (or a lower figure) strictly from that cost differential, stripping out 'conservation-incentive pricing' rationale, is a technically achievable fix and tracks Capistrano's core holding that tiered rates must track cost-of-service, not policy-driven demand suppression untethered from cost.

Curing §6(b)(2): redirecting the 55% low-income rebate spending off the surcharge-restricted fund (to general fund, grants, or a separately voter-approved tax) removes the cross-subsidy defect; retaining recycled-water debt service and conservation-education spending is more defensible since both plausibly relate to serving/managing the high-tier demand that generates the marginal cost.

Whether a fresh Art. XIII D §6(a) notice-and-majority-protest proceeding is legally *required* turns on whether the corrected rate counts as a 'new or increased' fee under Gov. Code §53750(h)(1) — a same-or-lower dollar charge with only a corrected cost study and revised fund-use might arguably not be an 'increase' requiring redo, but a wholesale re-derivation of methodology invalidated in litigation invites courts/agencies to treat it as a fresh 'imposition' out of caution (cf. Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil treating property-related water fees as fully subject to Prop 218 procedure).

Prop 26 voter-approval exposure is comparatively low if the fix succeeds: Cal. Const. art. XIII C, §1(e)(2) exempts fees for a specific benefit not exceeding reasonable cost, and the agency bears the burden of proof — a properly cost-capped, non-redistributive surcharge should fit that exemption and avoid tax/2/3-vote characterization, whereas an uncured version risks exactly that reclassification.

Practical/administrative incentive is separate from legal necessity: agencies redesigning a successfully-challenged rate structure commonly re-run the full notice/protest process anyway (clean record, insulation from renewed suit, refund-exposure management under Plantier v. Ramona Mun. Water Dist.), even where the constitutional text might not strictly compel it for a decrease-only change.
estimate.what_evidence_would_change_this: Would shift materially with: (1) the text of Gov. Code §53750(h)(1)/(2) and any case law construing what counts as an 'increased' fee versus a mere reallocation/re-justification of an unchanged or lowered charge; (2) whether the district's enabling ordinance or the original Prop 218 notice already reserved authority to reallocate restricted-fund spending without new notice; (3) post-Capistrano appellate or trial-court guidance (including the Capistrano remand record itself) on whether a agency curing a §6(b) defect via recalculated cost study must redo the full §6(a) hearing or can do so by resolution; (4) confirmation of the current $0.85 figure's actual relation to the 1.6x marginal-cost ceiling (if $0.85 already sits at or below cost, a pure re-derivation may not even change the charged amount, weakening the case for a required new hearing)."
